---
title: 
format: html
editor: visual
---

```{r}

```

only output is displayed).

```{r}
library("dplyr") #ggplot, cor(), cor.test(), linear model
library("tidyverse") #for data preparation and manipulation
```

```{r}
#read dataset into R & view 
#assessmentdf <-read.csv("studentAssessment.csv")

view(assessmentdf)

glimpse(assessmentdf) #to check data types
```

```{r}
#Transform "score" from character to numeric in order to run correlation and regression

assessmentdf$score <- as.numeric(assessmentdf$score)
  
  

glimpse(assessmentdf)

#Decided to rename several of the columns to improve readability to support clarity

assessmentdf <- assessmentdf  |>
  rename(
    assessment_id = id_assessment, 
    student_id = id_student, 
    banked = is_banked)
     #this column was renamed to reflect the metric being measured. The variable is not the date, but the number of days an assessment was submitted, after the module began. 

assessmentdf <- assessmentdf  |>
  rename(number_of_days = date_submitted)

view(assessmentdf)


```

```{r}
#NAs removed
#Submission timing was grouped into weekly intervals to reduce overplotting and improve interpretability. A reproducible random sample was used for visualization, with linear trends estimated on individual-level data.

set.seed(42)

plot_df <- assessmentdf %>%
  # 1. Remove missing values FIRST
  filter(
    !is.na(number_of_days),
    !is.na(score)
  ) %>%
  
  # 2. Create week bins to make the data easier to read on the scatterplot
  mutate(
    week_bin = floor(number_of_days / 7)
  ) %>%
  
  # 3. Then sample, again, so the scatterpoints are easier to read.
  sample_n(min(5000, nrow(.)))

# 4. Plot
ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = week_bin, y = score)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3,
             position = position_jitter(width = 0.2)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    title = "Assessment Score vs Submission Timing",
    subtitle = "NAs removed before sampling (reproducible)",
    x = "Weeks Since Module Start",
    y = "Assessment Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#When I ran the initial cor(), I received NAs. 
#I decided to use complete.obs to tell R to only use rows where both variables are present, so I could get a correlation value
cor

cor(
  assessmentdf$number_of_days,
  assessmentdf$score,
  method = "spearman",
  use = "complete.obs"
)

assessmentdf |>
  dplyr::select(score, number_of_days) |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(score), !is.na(number_of_days)) |>
  cor(method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs") |>
  round(3)

#The correlation value is -0.04. This indicates, as does our scatterplot, there is almost no relationship between how many days into the module a student submits there assessment and their assessment score. 

```

```{r}
#While cor() gives mthe correlation coefficient, I also want a p-value and confidence interval. 
#Will run cor.test() for these values.

cor.test(assessmentdf$score, assessmentdf$number_of_days, 
         alternative = "two.sided",
         method = "pearson",
         conf.level = 0.95)

#This gives a p-value close to 0, which says that assuming there's no correlation between scores and number of days metrics, there's a 0% probability that we'd observe a correlation of r = 0.958, just due to random chance. 

```

```{r}
#Simple Liner Regression

lm1 <- lm(score ~ number_of_days, data = assessmentdf)

summary(lm1)

#Interpretation:
#Analyzing the (Intercept) estimate, a student who submits their assessment at the very start of the  module, is predicted to score about a 76.6%. 

#For each additional day later a student submits their assessment, their score decreases by 0.009 points on average. Again, the number of days a student has access to the learning module, shows little correlation to their assessment score. 

#Looking at the Multiple R-squared, rounded to 3 decimal places, the value is 0.001. this means .1%, again, that this model's variables have no predictive power.

#If I had more time I would test other independent variables such as, students' education level, gender. I would use a different statistical tests such as T-test or ANOVA. 

```
